display_name: The /32 'local' route is the device's own interface IP, not a connected subnet — confusing them leads to wrong reachability conclusions
context: Operator checks the routing table and sees both 'C' (connected) and 'L' (local) entries for the same interface
observation: The routing table shows two entries for each interface: a connected route (C) with the subnet prefix and mask, and a local route (L) with a /32 host mask for the interface IP address
naive_interpretation: There are duplicate or redundant routes in the table — the 'L' (local) route is just another way of showing the connected subnet, or it is an extra route that can be removed
correct_interpretation: Connected (C) and local (L) routes serve fundamentally different purposes. The connected route (e.g., 'C 10.1.1.0/24 via Vlan100') tells the router that the entire subnet is reachable via that interface — any IP in the 10.1.1.0/24 range will be ARP-resolved on that interface. The local route (e.g., 'L 10.1.1.1/32 via Vlan100') is a host route for the device's own interface IP address — it tells the routing process that traffic destined TO 10.1.1.1 is for the device itself (deliver to CPU, don't forward). The local route always has a /32 mask and always has AD 0 — it cannot be overridden by any other routing protocol. Confusing these two route types leads to incorrect reachability analysis: the connected route determines what the device can REACH (other hosts in the subnet); the local route determines what is addressed TO the device itself. Removing or misinterpreting either one changes the forwarding behavior. In IOS-XR, the local route appears as 'L' with AD 0/metric 0; in IOS-XE and NX-OS, it also appears with the 'L' code.
why: Before IOS 15.0 and NX-OS 6.x, local routes were hidden — the routing table showed only the connected route; the introduction of explicit 'L' entries confused operators who had never seen them before

The local route is what triggers the device to process traffic as 'for me' vs 'to be forwarded' — without it, packets addressed to the interface IP would be forwarded out the connected interface as if destined for another host

In longest-prefix-match routing, the /32 local route always wins over the /24 connected route for traffic destined to the device's own IP — this is the mechanism that ensures self-addressed traffic reaches the CPU

Policy-based routing, route-maps, and PBR can match on connected routes — operators who don't understand the distinction sometimes write PBR rules that inadvertently match the local route and redirect traffic destined for the router itself

confirmation_commands.purpose: Display only connected routes (the subnets reachable via each interface) — local routes are excluded from this view
Display only local routes (/32 entries for the device's own interface IPs)
Full routing table showing both C (connected) and L (local) entries — verify that each interface has both and they are not being confused
Display connected routes separately from local routes for clear identification
Display connected subnet routes to distinguish from local host routes

traps: Treating the /32 local route as a connected subnet and concluding that the device has a /32 directly connected network — the /32 is a host route for the device's own IP, not a reachable subnet
Thinking the 'L' route is redundant with the 'C' route and trying to remove or suppress it — local routes are essential for the device to receive traffic addressed to its own interfaces
Using 'show ip route connected' to verify reachability to a specific IP and not finding the device's own interface IP — own-IP is in 'show ip route local', not 'connected'

In route redistribution, accidentally redistributing local routes (/32s) into a routing protocol — this advertises the device's interface IPs as host routes to the rest of the network, which may or may not be intended
Confusing the AD (administrative distance) of local (0) vs connected (0) — both have AD 0, but local routes have a /32 mask that gives them longest-prefix-match priority for self-addressed traffic
